title: Recurrent lightheadedness on standing in a polymedicated older man

chart.oneLiner: 75M with hypertension, type 2 diabetes (with known peripheral neuropathy), BPH, and a recent atrial-fibrillation diagnosis presents after feeling lightheaded and briefly passing out when standing from a chair; he is on multiple BP-lowering drugs plus a new rate-controlling agent, and has been eating and drinking poorly after a viral gastroenteritis.

opening: Every time I stand up I get dizzy, and this time I went right down to the floor.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 75-year-old man, comfortable and alert when seated, embarrassed and a bit frightened by the fall. Good historian but soft-spoken; lives alone since his wife died.

HPI: Lightheadedness specifically on STANDING for the past week, several times a day, each time a few seconds of grey-out that clears within a minute once he sits or lies down. Today he stood up from the kitchen chair, felt the room swim, and briefly passed out — a neighbor saw him crumple slowly to the floor, no jerking, no tongue-biting, no incontinence, awake and oriented within a minute (only if asked about the fall itself / witnesses / seizure features). He is NEVER dizzy lying flat or sitting still. NO chest pain, NO palpitations or racing heart, NO dizziness with exertion, NO breathlessness (only if asked). He had a vomiting-and-diarrhea illness about ten days ago and since then has barely eaten or drunk — 'nothing stays appetizing' — and his urine has been dark and scant (volume depletion; only if asked about intake / GI symptoms). NO black or bloody stools, NO vomiting blood (only if asked).
MEDICATION CHANGES (reveal only if asked about recent medication changes): two weeks ago his cardiologist started METOPROLOL for newly found atrial fibrillation, and at the same visit his thiazide dose was increased for blood pressure. He sometimes takes an extra tamsulosin at night and an over-the-counter diphenhydramine to sleep.
PMH: hypertension; type 2 diabetes mellitus (~12 years, with documented peripheral neuropathy — numb feet); benign prostatic hyperplasia; atrial fibrillation (new, two weeks ago). MEDS: hydrochlorothiazide (recently increased), lisinopril, amlodipine, metoprolol tartrate (new), tamsulosin, metformin, apixaban, occasional over-the-counter diphenhydramine for sleep. SOCIAL: lives alone, no caregiver, climbs a flight of stairs to his apartment; former 40-pack-year smoker, quit 8 years ago; minimal alcohol. FAMILY: noncontributory. ROS: as above; weight down a few pounds; no fevers; no focal weakness or numbness beyond his long-standing foot numbness.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: well-appearing, comfortable seated, no acute distress.
- Mucous membranes: dry; skin turgor reduced (only if examined).
- Vitals SEATED: BP 126/76, HR 58. STANDING (only if orthostatic vitals are checked): BP 92/58 with NO appropriate rise in heart rate (HR 60) and reproduction of his lightheadedness — a positive orthostatic drop with a BLUNTED compensatory tachycardia.
- Cardiac: irregularly irregular pulse, rate ~58, no murmur, no S3 (only if examined).
- Neuro: alert and oriented; no focal motor deficit; reduced sensation to monofilament in both feet (diabetic neuropathy); gait cautious but non-ataxic (only if examined).
- Rectal: brown stool, no melena, NEGATIVE occult blood (only if examined).

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the recent metoprolol start, the thiazide increase, the extra tamsulosin/diphenhydramine, the poor intake, or the recent gastroenteritis unless asked about medication changes, intake, or GI symptoms.
- Do NOT volunteer the seizure-negative / witnessed-fall details unless asked about the fall, loss of consciousness, witnesses, or seizure features.
- The orthostatic vitals (including the blunted heart-rate response) are revealed only if the student specifically checks orthostatic vitals.
- Reveal exam findings only when the relevant maneuver is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.BMP: Na 133, K 3.1, Cl 96, HCO3 30, BUN 38, Cr 1.5 (baseline 1.0). Anion gap = 133 − (96 + 30) = 7 (low-normal). Picture: prerenal acute kidney injury (BUN:Cr 25) with a contraction/hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis and thiazide-driven hypokalemia and hyponatremia — all consistent with diuretic use plus volume depletion.
labs.CBC: WBC 7.2, Hgb 14.1, Hct 42%, Plt 240 — no anemia and no evidence of occult GI bleed.
labs.TSH: 2.1 mIU/L — normal; thyroid is not the cause of the bradycardia.
labs.Troponin: High-sensitivity troponin negative on serial draws — no acute myocardial injury.
labs.Orthostatics: Seated BP 126/76, HR 58 → standing BP 92/58, HR 60. SBP falls 34 mmHg (>20) and DBP falls 18 mmHg (>10) with symptom reproduction and NO compensatory rise in heart rate — a positive orthostatic drop with a blunted/absent reflex tachycardia.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no organized P waves) at a CONTROLLED-to-SLOW ventricular rate ~55–60 bpm; first-degree-equivalent slowing from AV-nodal blockade; narrow QRS; no acute ST-segment elevation or depression; prolonged but non-malignant QT in the setting of hypokalemia and diphenhydramine.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: atrial fibrillation at a slow/controlled ventricular response (~55–60 bpm), narrow QRS, no acute ischemia. Self-host with appropriate credit.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing atrial fibrillation with a slow/controlled ventricular response
searchTerms: atrial fibrillation slow ventricular response ECG
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 75-year-old man on a new beta-blocker for atrial fibrillation presents after lightheadedness and a fall; his pulse is irregular and slow.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Single well-circumscribed pulmonary nodule (~1.4 cm) in the right mid lung zone; lungs otherwise clear; normal cardiomediastinal silhouette; no effusion or free air.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: single well-circumscribed ~1.4 cm pulmonary nodule in the mid lung zone, otherwise normal film. Self-host with appropriate credit.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing a solitary pulmonary nodule
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 75-year-old former heavy smoker has a chest film that incidentally shows a rounded shadow in the lung.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this older man's syncope (note your leading diagnosis). As part of your answer, name at least three distinct mechanisms/etiologies of your leading diagnosis in a patient like this.
steps.rubric: Orthostatic hypotension (leading,5; medication- and volume-related). Cardiac/bradyarrhythmic syncope — must-not-miss given new AF, new beta-blocker, and a slow pulse (CANT-MISS,4). Occult GI bleed / anemia as a cause of hypovolemia (CANT-MISS,3). Structural cardiac disease / aortic stenosis (CANT-MISS,2). Seizure vs true syncope (core,1). Vasovagal / situational (core,1). Named leading dx w/ justification — symptoms only on standing, documented drop, polypharmacy, volume loss (core,3). Names ≥3 mechanisms of orthostatic hypotension — e.g. volume depletion, drug-induced (diuretic/antihypertensive/alpha-blocker/nodal blocker), and autonomic/neurogenic failure (e.g. diabetic autonomic neuropathy) (core,6). PENALTY: anchoring on a benign medication side effect and dismissing cardiac/bradyarrhythmic syncope in an older man with new AF (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Orthostatic hypotension (symptoms only on standing, a documented orthostatic drop, recent volume loss, and heavy antihypertensive/diuretic polypharmacy). Must-not-miss alternatives to exclude: cardiac/bradyarrhythmic syncope (he has NEW atrial fibrillation, a NEW beta-blocker, and a slow pulse — bradycardia or pauses could cause syncope), occult GI bleed/anemia driving the hypovolemia, and structural disease such as aortic stenosis. Also consider seizure (the witnessed account argues against it) and vasovagal/situational syncope. Three distinct mechanisms of orthostatic hypotension here: (1) intravascular VOLUME DEPLETION (recent gastroenteritis, poor intake, prerenal labs); (2) DRUG-INDUCED impaired vasoconstriction/heart-rate response (thiazide, lisinopril, amlodipine, tamsulosin alpha-blockade, and the new metoprolol blunting reflex tachycardia, plus anticholinergic diphenhydramine); and (3) NEUROGENIC/autonomic failure from long-standing diabetic autonomic neuropathy — supported by the blunted compensatory tachycardia on standing.

criticalActions.item: Cardiac / bradyarrhythmic syncope (new AF, new beta-blocker, slow pulse)

coreActions.item: Named leading dx w/ justification — symptoms on standing, documented drop, polypharmacy, volume loss
Names ≥3 mechanisms of orthostatic hypotension (volume depletion; drug-induced; neurogenic/autonomic e.g. diabetic neuropathy)

penalties.item: Anchors on a benign medication side effect and dismisses cardiac/bradyarrhythmic syncope in an older man with new AF

mustNotMiss: Cardiac / bradyarrhythmic syncope (new AF, new beta-blocker, slow pulse)

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Orthostatic vital signs WITH the heart-rate response (CANT-MISS,4). 12-lead EKG to characterize the rhythm / exclude bradyarrhythmia given new AF and a new beta-blocker (CANT-MISS,4). BMP for electrolytes/renal function (2). CBC to exclude anemia/occult bleed (1). Careful medication reconciliation (2). Troponin and/or TSH; CXR (bonus,1). PENALTY: skipping orthostatics or the medication reconciliation (-2); ignoring the EKG/rhythm in a patient with new AF on a new beta-blocker (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: orthostatic vital signs including the heart-rate response; a 12-lead EKG to characterize the rhythm and exclude a bradyarrhythmia (he has new AF and a new beta-blocker with a slow pulse); BMP; CBC; Glucose; Troponin; TSH; CXR. Perform a careful medication reconciliation.

criticalActions.item: 12-lead EKG to characterize rhythm / exclude bradyarrhythmia

penalties.item: Skipping orthostatics or the medication reconciliation
Ignoring the EKG/rhythm in a patient with new AF on a new beta-blocker

requiredTests: 12-lead EKG to characterize rhythm / exclude bradyarrhythmia
contraindicatedTests: Skipping orthostatics or the medication reconciliation
Ignoring the EKG/rhythm in a patient with new AF on a new beta-blocker

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is THIS patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and state how they change your assessment of his syncope.
steps.rubric: Findings = atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, absent organized P waves) at a SLOW/controlled ventricular response ~55–60 bpm, narrow QRS, no acute ischemia; the slow rate reflects AV-nodal blockade from the new metoprolol (compounded by hypokalemia). CREDIT: identifies atrial fibrillation (5); recognizes the ventricular rate is slow/over-controlled and attributes it to the new beta-blocker, raising bradyarrhythmia/pauses as a contributor to syncope (6); systematic read with no acute ischemia (2). PENALTY: mistaking AF for normal sinus rhythm or for a primary high-grade AV block, or missing that the rate is inappropriately slow for a syncopal patient (-3).
keyFindings: atrial fibrillation with absent organized P waves and an irregularly irregular rhythm
slow/over-controlled ventricular response (~55–60) attributable to the new beta-blocker
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation with a slow/controlled ventricular response (~55–60 bpm) from AV-nodal blockade — an over-controlled rate that may contribute to syncope.
commonMistakes: calling an irregularly irregular rhythm normal sinus rhythm
failing to note that the ventricular rate is inappropriately slow in a syncopal patient on a new beta-blocker
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation — irregularly irregular with no organized P waves — at a SLOW, over-controlled ventricular response of ~55–60 bpm, narrow QRS, no acute ST/T changes. The slow rate is the footprint of his new metoprolol (with hypokalemia contributing), and it reframes the case: he is on a NEW AV-nodal blocker that has over-controlled his rate, so a bradyarrhythmia or drug-induced pauses could be contributing to his syncope and his blunted compensatory tachycardia on standing. This is the patient's own study, not a benign incidental — it tells me to lighten or hold rate control and consider rhythm monitoring while I treat the orthostatic hypotension.

criticalActions: Atrial fibrillation with a slow/controlled ventricular response (~55–60 bpm) from AV-nodal blockade — an over-controlled rate that may contribute to syncope.
coreActions: atrial fibrillation with absent organized P waves and an irregularly irregular rhythm
slow/over-controlled ventricular response (~55–60) attributable to the new beta-blocker

penalties.label: calls the study normal sinus / misses that the rate is inappropriately slow

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = solitary pulmonary nodule (~1.4 cm, well-circumscribed, right mid lung zone), lungs otherwise clear. CREDIT: identifies the solitary pulmonary nodule (6); recommends comparison with prior imaging and structured follow-up given his heavy smoking history (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal / dismissing the nodule as a nipple shadow without confirmation (-3).
keyFindings: recommends comparison with priors / structured follow-up given smoking history
commonMistakes: dismissing the nodule as a nipple shadow without confirmation
missing it on an otherwise unremarkable film
steps.idealAnswer: Solitary pulmonary nodule (~1.4 cm, well-circumscribed) in the right mid lung zone; lungs otherwise clear with a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette. This is incidental to his syncope but important in a former 40-pack-year smoker — recommend comparison with any prior imaging and a structured nodule follow-up pathway (e.g. CT and Fleischner-based surveillance).

coreActions: recommends comparison with priors / structured follow-up given smoking history

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the nodule

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the positive orthostatics, the multi-system labs, and THIS patient's EKG, what is your diagnosis and the contributors? Commit to the most likely answer, name the test that discriminates it from the leading alternative, and state any residual uncertainty.
steps.rubric: Orthostatic hypotension as the cause of the syncope (committed), multifactorial: volume depletion (gastroenteritis/poor intake, prerenal AKI with BUN:Cr 25, hypochloremic contraction alkalosis, thiazide-driven hyponatremia/hypokalemia) PLUS polypharmacy (thiazide, lisinopril, amlodipine, tamsulosin, new metoprolol, anticholinergic diphenhydramine) PLUS a neurogenic component from diabetic autonomic neuropathy (blunted reflex tachycardia). Discriminator: the orthostatic vitals reproduce symptoms positionally while the EKG/telemetry addresses the competing bradyarrhythmic cause — telemetry/monitoring is what discriminates a primary bradyarrhythmia from positional OH. Residual uncertainty: the over-controlled AF on a new beta-blocker means a contributing bradyarrhythmia is not fully excluded until monitored off/with reduced nodal blockade.
steps.idealAnswer: Diagnosis: Orthostatic hypotension causing his syncope — and it is MULTIFACTORIAL. Contributors: (1) volume depletion (recent gastroenteritis and poor intake, with prerenal acute kidney injury — BUN 38/Cr 1.5, BUN:Cr 25 — plus a hypochloremic contraction alkalosis and thiazide-driven hyponatremia [Na 133] and hypokalemia [K 3.1]); (2) polypharmacy (thiazide, lisinopril, amlodipine, the alpha-blocker tamsulosin, the new metoprolol, and anticholinergic diphenhydramine); and (3) a neurogenic component from long-standing diabetic autonomic neuropathy, supported by the blunted compensatory tachycardia on standing. The discriminating data: the orthostatic vital signs reproduce his symptoms positionally and a negative troponin and slow but organized AF make an acute coronary or tachyarrhythmic cause unlikely — but the EKG shows his rate is OVER-CONTROLLED on the new beta-blocker, so the test that truly discriminates a contributing primary bradyarrhythmia from pure positional OH is continuous telemetry/rhythm monitoring. Residual uncertainty: I commit to orthostatic hypotension, but I cannot fully exclude a contributing bradyarrhythmia/pause until he is monitored with reduced AV-nodal blockade.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan? Include your disposition decision — is it safe to discharge this man who lives alone, and if so under what conditions?
steps.rubric: Per MGH syncope (orthostatic): identify and treat the underlying cause / volume repletion with IV isotonic fluids and correct the electrolytes — replace potassium, hold the thiazide (CANT-MISS,6); medication reconciliation — stop/reduce the offending drugs, hold or reduce the new metoprolol given the over-controlled rate, hold tamsulosin and diphenhydramine, reassess antihypertensives (CANT-MISS,6); non-pharmacologic measures — slow position changes, compression stockings, physical counter-pressure (3); liberalize salt/fluids and educate (2); telemetry/rhythm monitoring before deciding on rate control given the over-controlled AF (3); midodrine/fludrocortisone only if refractory after volume/med correction (2); SAFE DISPOSITION — given he lives alone, fell, and has a still-incomplete cardiac evaluation, ADMIT or observe with telemetry, repeat orthostatics, PT/falls and home-safety evaluation, med-reconciliation, and arrange the lung-nodule follow-up before discharge; do NOT discharge home alone the same day until orthostatics resolve and a bradyarrhythmia is excluded (3). PENALTY: discharging this fall patient home alone without addressing the over-controlled rhythm or arranging follow-up (-4); missing hypovolemia/occult bleed as a driver (-3); adding a pressor (midodrine/fludrocortisone) before correcting volume and offending meds (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Stabilize and treat the cause: IV isotonic fluids for volume repletion, replace potassium and correct the electrolyte derangements, and HOLD the thiazide. Medication reconciliation is central — hold/reduce the new metoprolol (his AF rate is over-controlled and contributing to the blunted tachycardia and possible bradyarrhythmia), hold the alpha-blocker tamsulosin and the anticholinergic diphenhydramine, and reassess lisinopril/amlodipine. Non-pharmacologic measures: slow position changes, compression stockings, physical counter-pressure maneuvers, and liberalize salt and fluids with education. Put him on TELEMETRY/rhythm monitoring before re-deciding rate control, given the over-controlled AF. Reserve midodrine or fludrocortisone for refractory orthostatic hypotension only after volume and medications are corrected. DISPOSITION: he lives ALONE, had a witnessed fall, and his cardiac evaluation is not yet complete, so this is NOT a same-day discharge home alone. Admit or place in observation with telemetry; repeat orthostatic vitals after fluids and med changes, obtain a physical-therapy falls assessment and home-safety evaluation, complete medication reconciliation, and arrange close primary-care and cardiology follow-up plus a structured pulmonary-nodule follow-up pathway. Discharge only once orthostatics have resolved, a contributing bradyarrhythmia is excluded on monitoring, and safe follow-up and home supports are in place.

criticalActions.item: Volume repletion (IV isotonic fluids), correct electrolytes / replace potassium, hold thiazide
Medication reconciliation — hold/reduce new metoprolol and offending drugs (tamsulosin, diphenhydramine), reassess antihypertensives

coreActions.item: Non-pharmacologic measures (compression, slow position changes, counter-pressure)
Liberalize salt and fluids with education
Telemetry / rhythm monitoring before re-deciding rate control
Midodrine/fludrocortisone only if refractory after volume/med correction

Safe disposition — admit/observe given lives alone + fall + incomplete cardiac workup; repeat orthostatics, PT falls/home-safety eval, med reconciliation, arrange nodule + cardiology follow-up before discharge

penalties.item: Discharging this fall patient home alone without addressing the over-controlled rhythm or arranging follow-up
Missing hypovolemia / occult bleed as a driver
Adding a pressor (midodrine/fludrocortisone) before correcting volume and offending meds

physicalExamMappings.finding: Vitals SEATED: BP 126/76, HR 58. STANDING (orthostatics checked): BP 92/58 with NO appropriate rise in heart rate (HR 60) and reproduction of lightheadedness — a positive orthostatic drop with a blunted compensatory tachycardia.
Cardiac: irregularly irregular pulse, rate ~58, no murmur, no S3.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: No murmur appreciated; rhythm to be characterized on EKG.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Mucous membranes dry; skin turgor reduced — consistent with volume depletion.
Neuro: alert and oriented; no focal motor deficit; reduced sensation to monofilament in both feet (diabetic peripheral neuropathy); gait cautious but non-ataxic.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 75M with hypertension, type 2 diabetes (with autonomic neuropathy), BPH, and NEW atrial fibrillation presents after lightheadedness and a witnessed fall on standing, on heavy antihypertensive/diuretic polypharmacy plus a newly started beta-blocker, after a recent gastroenteritis with poor intake. The diagnosis is multifactorial Orthostatic hypotension — driven by volume depletion (prerenal AKI, contraction alkalosis, thiazide-related hyponatremia/hypokalemia), polypharmacy, and diabetic autonomic neuropathy — reached while excluding the competing must-not-miss of cardiac/bradyarrhythmic syncope. The patient's own EKG (integrated) shows atrial fibrillation with an over-controlled slow ventricular response from the new beta-blocker, which both raises and is folded into the bradyarrhythmia question and the disposition decision.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Vitals SEATED: BP 126/76, HR 58. STANDING (orthostatics checked): BP 92/58 with NO appropriate rise in heart rate (HR 60) and reproduction of lightheadedness — a positive orthostatic drop with a blunted compensatory tachycardia.
Cardiac: irregularly irregular pulse, rate ~58, no murmur, no S3.
Mucous membranes dry; skin turgor reduced — consistent with volume depletion.
Neuro: alert and oriented; no focal motor deficit; reduced sensation to monofilament in both feet (diabetic peripheral neuropathy); gait cautious but non-ataxic.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals (note the slow pulse in a syncopal man on a new beta-blocker).
Elicit the focused history — recent medication changes, poor intake, gastroenteritis, the witnessed fall, and social supports — only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam, especially orthostatic vitals WITH the heart-rate response, and characterize the irregular pulse.
Build a differential led by Orthostatic hypotension while explicitly excluding cardiac/bradyarrhythmic syncope, occult GI bleed, and structural disease, and naming ≥3 mechanisms of OH.
Order a focused workup; read the patient's OWN EKG (integrated) showing over-controlled atrial fibrillation; read the CXR nodule; integrate the multi-system labs.

Commit to multifactorial Orthostatic hypotension, name telemetry as the discriminator from a primary bradyarrhythmia, and manage with volume/electrolyte correction, medication reconciliation, non-pharmacologic measures, and a SAFE disposition for a man who lives alone.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on a benign medication side effect and dismissing cardiac/bradyarrhythmic syncope
Reading the EKG as normal sinus or missing that the AF rate is inappropriately slow
Discharging a fall patient who lives alone before orthostatics resolve and a bradyarrhythmia is excluded
Adding a pressor before correcting volume and offending medications
managementPearls: Volume repletion (IV isotonic fluids), correct electrolytes / replace potassium, hold thiazide
Medication reconciliation — hold/reduce new metoprolol and offending drugs, reassess antihypertensives
criticalWorkupItems: 12-lead EKG to characterize rhythm / exclude bradyarrhythmia
criticalInterpretationItems: Atrial fibrillation with a slow/over-controlled ventricular response from the new beta-blocker — a contributor to syncope, not a benign incidental.
Solitary pulmonary nodule (~1.4 cm) — recommend comparison with priors / structured follow-up given heavy smoking history.
criticalManagementItems: Volume repletion (IV isotonic fluids), correct electrolytes / replace potassium, hold thiazide
Medication reconciliation — hold/reduce new metoprolol and offending drugs, reassess antihypertensives
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with a slow/controlled ventricular response (~55–60 bpm) from AV-nodal blockade — an over-controlled rate that may contribute to syncope.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with a slow/controlled ventricular response (~55–60 bpm) from AV-nodal blockade — an over-controlled rate that may contribute to syncope.
imageInterpretationPearls: When the read is the patient's OWN tracing, integrate it into the syncope story — an over-controlled AF rate on a new beta-blocker is a contributor, not a bystander.
An incidental abnormality (a lung nodule) is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance and arrange follow-up.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Elicits the patient's concerns / ICE (e.g. fear of falling, living alone)

libraryUpgrade.imagePolicy: integrated-read EKG (patient's own study) + pathology-forward standalone CXR
